workMixDesign.sourceDerivedSummary: NBA team beat reporting is an in-person deadline job built from repeated arena access, source conversations, observation, note discipline, and fast digital filing. Source material describes reporters attending shootaround and games, talking to coaches and players before and after games, transcribing and organizing interviews, watching injuries and rotations, and writing instant stories after the buzzer. The upgraded simulator therefore mixes physical arena observation, spoken availability, Slack/editor communication, source sorting, and deadline writing rather than treating the role as generic prose composition.

sourceEvidence: Beat reporters are physically at games, practices, shootarounds, media entrances, courtside areas, and postgame access spaces.
Courtside and beat reporting source material emphasizes notebook/recorder use, observing player movement, and working from the arena media table.
physicalProceduralTool.simulatorImplication: Include a playable arena observation scene where the student inspects visible court/media-row evidence and records a caveated observation for the later story.

sourceEvidence: Reporters work in Slack/editor channels, CMS drafts, live box scores, notes, transcripts, injury reports, and social rumor streams.
The same-day game article depends on turning digital and observed inputs into publishable copy under a clock.
digitalToolArtifactWork.simulatorImplication: Use realistic document, Slack, kanban, and CMS-style surfaces with task-specific reference tabs.

sourceEvidence: The reporter must decide what is newsworthy, what is verified, what needs attribution, and what should be held back.
Sources emphasize separating observation from confirmed information and choosing a focused angle under deadline.
cognitiveAnalysisDecision.simulatorImplication: Keep the lead-angle pivot, note classification, and story-structure choices as explicit judgment moments.

sourceEvidence: Beat reporters write recaps, follow-up angles, source notes, and deadline stories after interviews and games.
Source material repeatedly describes writing instant stories right after the buzzer and revising with editor direction.
writtenDocumentationArtifact.simulatorImplication: Require a reporting plan, observation note, note rationale, and one deadline article.

sourceEvidence: The role involves asking coaches and players concise questions during media availability and scrums.
Beat reporting depends on source relationships, respectful follow-ups, and extracting usable attribution without leading.
spokenInterpersonalCommunication.simulatorImplication: Use in-person voice scenes for coach availability and the player scrum, with prep notes visible.

sourceEvidence: Game-night reporting includes waiting for media doors, watching warmups, monitoring a live game, and reacting to late updates.
The reporter works across pregame, live game, postgame access, and filing windows.
passiveMonitoringWaitingContextSwitching.simulatorImplication: Represent waiting and monitoring through live game notes, app-specific updates, and deadline constraints, not passive click-through narration.

workMixDesign.simulatorPlan: The active flow starts with a bounded newsroom angle choice, then has the student build a reporting plan from visible source material, perform an in-arena observation, ask coach and player questions in person, capture organized possession timeline notes, and assemble one deadline article from supported paragraph choices. The physical scene is intentionally secondary but meaningful; source judgment, story structure, and communication remain the dominant experience.
workMixDesign.activeSceneMixRationale: Active scenes include one MCQ pivot, one structured reporting plan, one physical playground, one possession timeline, two in-person voice conversations, and one article assembly task. That balance mirrors the source-derived job mix for an entry-level NBA beat reporter while keeping the article-writing load appropriate for high school students.

intro.title: Welcome to Full Court Wire
intro.content: You are an entry-level NBA beat reporter at Full Court Wire. Tonight you are covering the fictional Harbor City Cyclones from inside the arena: choose a story angle you can prove, use the right materials at the right time, watch Reed in person, ask coach and player questions, sort your notes, and send an article draft your editor can trust before the deadline.
intro.imageBrief: First-person view from an NBA arena media-row work table before tipoff. A laptop shows a CMS draft, live box score, Slack editor thread, and credential badge for Harbor City Arena; a notebook and recorder sit beside the keyboard while the court glows in the background.

assignment_recap.title: 4:18 PM Editor Recap
assignment_recap.content: Before you choose your story angle, read the facts. Make sure you understand what we know about Reed, why this game matters, and what you still need to confirm with public quotes.
assignment_recap.imageBrief: Arena media room laptop with a short editor Slack recap visible, a notebook, phone recorder, credential badge, and the court tunnel in the background. Keep all screen text generic or unreadable; the actual recap is rendered by the simulator UI.

memoryCard.title: Game-night frame
bullets: Your game article is due at 11:10 PM ET.
Malik Reed is returning from a right ankle sprain.
The question is whether Coach Harris can trust Reed in the final minutes.

coworkerRecap.fallback: I do not want to invent extra context here. Hold onto the editor frame and use the task tabs when you need details.

turns.coworkerLine: Malik Reed is a veteran guard returning from a right ankle sprain. The team lists him available tonight, and no minutes limit is listed.
answerFacts: Malik Reed is returning from a right ankle sprain.
The team lists Malik Reed as available tonight.
No minutes limit is listed for Malik Reed.

turns.coworkerLine: Harbor City's late-game lineups have been unsettled without Reed. Denver's guards attack near the basket late, so the final minutes will show whether Coach Harris trusts Reed in that group.
answerFacts: Harbor City's late-game lineups have been unsettled without Reed.
Denver's guards attack near the basket late in games.
The final minutes will show whether Coach Harris trusts Reed in the late-game group.

turns.coworkerLine: The article is not just 'Reed is back.' It is whether his return changes who plays the final minutes, and what Coach Harris or Reed can say publicly and by name.
answerFacts: The article should not stop at Malik Reed being back.
The reporting question is whether Reed's return changes who plays the final minutes.
Coach Harris and Malik Reed can provide named, public support for role and health claims.

assignment_brief.title: 4:22 PM Assignment Desk
assignment_brief.content: You are at the arena media table before warmups. Tipoff is still ahead, and Tessa's assignment thread is open. First, confirm what the newsroom wants, use only checked pregame materials, and decide what you need to watch when Reed steps on the floor.

slackMessages.content: Tonight's working question is whether Reed's return changes the players used late in the game. Watch warmups, ask Coach Harris about late-game choices, and keep the story tied to what we can check in the building.
Also: treat social media rumors as unconfirmed unless a team, agent, league, or source willing to be named gives us something real. Do not make a social rumor the main story.

emails.subject: Official injury report - Harbor City vs. Denver
emails.content:
Official injury report, 4:05 PM ET update

Malik Reed - Available (returning from right ankle sprain)
Minutes limit - None listed

Use the official status only as listed. Any other health or readiness claim needs a named source you can quote.

emails.subject: Arena media schedule - Cyclones vs. Denver
emails.content:
4:52 PM - Coach Len Harris pregame availability, interview-room platform
7:10 PM - Tipoff
Postgame - Baseline scrum opens after a short locker-room cool-down period
Postgame note - Malik Reed is expected at the baseline scrum if he closes or scores 20+

metrics.target: Same core group in late possessions
metrics.actual: Four different closing groups in the last five close games
Denver's lead guards average 8.7 fourth-quarter paint touches

socialPosts.content: Hearing Reed's return could be connected to bigger trade talks involving guards. If he closes tonight, watch the front-office story.
No official word, but the timing is interesting: Reed is back, scouts are in the building, and trade-deadline talk is everywhere.

cmsSidebarRules.label: Health language
cmsSidebarRules.detail: Do not write that Reed is fully healed, medically cleared, or back to normal unless Reed, team PR, or another source willing to be named says it.

cmsSidebarRules.label: Evidence groups
cmsSidebarRules.detail: Keep official status, your own observations, direct quotes, and unverified rumors separate in the draft.

cmsSidebarRules.label: Attribution bar
cmsSidebarRules.detail: Name the source for every health or role claim: team PR listed it, Coach Harris said it, Reed said it, or you observed it.

cmsSidebarRules.label: Hold unverified material
cmsSidebarRules.detail: Do not publish social media rumors or hallway talk unless a source willing to be named confirms them.

assignment_brief.imageBrief: Arena media room laptop with Slack assignment messages, official injury report, arena media schedule, stats dashboard, social media feed, CMS sidebar, deadline timer, and live game credential. The basketball court is visible through an open tunnel.

lead_angle.title: Reply With Your Reporting Angle
lead_angle.content: Use the laptop window below. First read Tessa's message in #nba-cyclones. Then open Email, Game Materials, and Social Media. Choose the reply that uses the strongest facts from those tabs. You have 25 minutes before pregame coach availability.
lead_angle.prompt: After checking the tabs, choose the reply you would send Tessa.

slackMessages.content: What is your working story angle before coach availability? Send the one you can actually prove tonight.

emails.subject: Official injury report - Harbor City vs. Denver
emails.content:
Official injury report, 4:05 PM ET update

Malik Reed - Available (returning from right ankle sprain)
Minutes limit - None listed

Use the official status only as listed. Any other health or readiness claim needs a named source you can quote.

metrics.target: Same core group in late possessions
metrics.actual: Four different closing groups in the last five close games
Denver's lead guards average 8.7 fourth-quarter paint touches

cmsSidebarRules.label: Health language
cmsSidebarRules.detail: Do not write that Reed is fully healed, medically cleared, or back to normal unless Reed, team PR, or another source willing to be named says it.

cmsSidebarRules.label: Evidence groups
cmsSidebarRules.detail: Keep official status, your own observations, direct quotes, and unverified rumors separate.

cmsSidebarRules.label: Attribution bar
cmsSidebarRules.detail: Name the source for health or role claims: team PR, Coach Harris, Reed, or your own observation.

cmsSidebarRules.label: Hold unverified material
cmsSidebarRules.detail: Do not publish social media rumors or hallway talk unless a source willing to be named confirms them.

socialPosts.content: Hearing Reed's return could be connected to bigger trade talks involving guards. If he closes tonight, watch the front-office story.
No official word, but the timing is interesting: Reed is back, scouts are in the building, and trade-deadline talk is everywhere.

lead_angle.imageBrief: Reporter notebook with three circled possible angles: injury return, closing lineup, trade rumor. A phone shows social posts while an arena clock counts down.

deskWorkDesign.workSurface: Slack message decision inside a cream app window

inputArtifacts.label: Tessa's editor recap
inputArtifacts.summary: Reed background, game stakes, and the reporting question

inputArtifacts.label: Reporting-angle tabs
inputArtifacts.summary: Official Reed status email, late-game stats, social media rumors, and CMS attribution rules

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Choose and send the most reportable angle to pursue from a finite editor prompt.
continuity: The selected reply appears in the redirect scene and sets the reporting plan frame.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: The scene requires the player to transform visible reporting inputs into a beat-reporter artifact instead of answering from memory.

options.label: I will report on Reed's return and the late-game lineup. I can prove it with official status, warmups, Coach Harris, and Reed postgame.
I will report the trade rumor because fan accounts are sharing it and it may get attention.
I will write a broad game preview so the story stays safe.
I will focus on Coach Harris's mood because his tension could add scene detail.

redirect_strong_angle.title: Angle Greenlit
redirect_strong_angle.content: Tessa likes the angle because you can support it with official status, what you see, coach availability, and Reed attribution. Now turn the angle into a reporting plan.

slackMessages.content: What is your working story angle before coach availability? Send the one you can actually prove tonight.
Good. Build the plan around what you can prove: official status, what you personally see, what Coach Harris can explain, and what Reed can say publicly and by name.

redirect_strong_angle.imageBrief: Slack thread in an NBA arena media room where the editor greenlights a focused Reed-return and closing-lineup angle.

redirect_rumor.title: Rumor Without Grounding
redirect_rumor.content: The rumor gets attention, but nobody with direct knowledge will put a name on it. Tessa redirects you so you protect credibility and report what you can actually prove tonight.

slackMessages.content: What is your working story angle before coach availability? Send the one you can actually prove tonight.
Monitor it, but do not make it your main story. If it is not confirmed by a source willing to be named, it stays out. Pivot back to Reed's usage and the final-minutes lineup.

redirect_rumor.imageBrief: Phone screen full of rumor posts beside a reporter notebook marked no confirmation. Editor Slack message says verify before amplifying.

redirect_generic.title: Too Broad for Deadline
redirect_generic.content: A broad preview would be accurate but forgettable. Tessa needs you to use your limited access on a sharper question that can hold up when the story is edited on deadline.

slackMessages.content: What is your working story angle before coach availability? Send the one you can actually prove tonight.
That is too wide for tonight. Narrow it: what does Reed's return change late, and what can you prove before you send the story?

redirect_generic.imageBrief: A generic game preview draft fading behind a sharper sticky note: Reed return plus closing lineup.

redirect_color.title: Color Needs a Point
redirect_color.content: Coach mood may make the scene vivid, but mood is not the story by itself. Use color only when it supports a decision or result you can prove.

slackMessages.content: What is your working story angle before coach availability? Send the one you can actually prove tonight.
Color supports the story; it is not the story. Pursue the Reed/closing-lineup question first, then use scene detail if it helps the reader.

redirect_color.imageBrief: Coach at podium in a tense pregame media room, with reporter notes connecting mood to lineup decisions rather than gossip.

reporting_plan.title: Pregame Reporting Plan
reporting_plan.content: A reporting plan is a short checklist. It helps you decide what to ask, what fact is safe to use, and what rumor or guess must stay out of the story. Click Source Notes first. Then return to Your Plan, scroll through all three rows, and fill each field. You are not writing the article yet.
reporting_plan.prompt: Use Source Notes to complete Row 1, Row 2, and Row 3. Submit will unlock when every field is filled.

appTabs.label: Source Notes
appTabs.content:
STORY FOCUS
- Tessa wants to know whether Malik Reed's return changes who Coach Harris trusts near the end of a close game.
- Your plan has three rows: one question for Coach Harris, one question for Reed after the game, and one safe-fact/unsafe-claim check.

USE FOR ROW 1: QUESTION FOR COACH HARRIS BEFORE THE GAME
- Ask what will decide whether Reed can play near the end of a close game.
- Helpful facts: Reed is available, no {{minutes limit}} is listed, and the Cyclones have used four different {{closing group}}s in the last five close games.

USE FOR ROW 2: QUESTION FOR REED AFTER THE GAME
- Ask Reed what he personally felt or noticed after returning from the ankle sprain.
- Reed may speak {{postgame}} if he plays near the end of the game or scores 20+ points.

USE FOR ROW 3: SAFE FACT AND UNSAFE CLAIM
- A safe fact is something the notes already support with a {{named source}}.
- Good facts: {{Team PR}} says Reed is available after a right ankle sprain; Team PR lists no cap on Reed's playing time; the arena media schedule says Coach Harris speaks at 4:52 PM.
- An unsafe {{claim}} is something no named source has confirmed yet.

USE FOR ROW 3: WHAT TO LEAVE OUT
- Do not say Reed is fully healed or back to normal unless Reed, Team PR, or another named source says it.
- Leave out social media rumors or hallway talk unless a source willing to be named confirms them.

reporting_plan.imageBrief: Document editor with a structured reporting plan, live stats, injury report, and a credentialed reporter notebook at an arena media table.

inputArtifacts.label: Source Notes
inputArtifacts.summary: Tessa's assignment, official Reed status, arena schedule, late-game context, and what not to publish yet.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Complete three required planning rows: a coach question, a Reed question, and a safe-fact/unsafe-claim row.
continuity: The plan appears in coach_availability through prepNoteKey and sets up later Reed questions.

rowGuides.title: Question for Coach Harris before the game
rowGuides.sourceHint: Ask Coach Harris what will decide whether Reed can play near the end of a close game.

need.label: Topic to ask about

question.placeholder: Write one fair question Coach Harris can answer before the game.

risk.label: Why this matters

rowGuides.title: Question for Reed after the game
rowGuides.sourceHint: Ask Reed what he personally felt or noticed after returning.

need.label: Topic to ask about
need.placeholder: Example: How Reed's ankle felt during a real game play.

question.label: Exact question for Reed
question.placeholder: Write one fair question Reed can answer from his own experience.

risk.label: Why this matters
risk.placeholder: Explain what this answer helps you learn for the story.

rowGuides.title: Safe fact and unsafe claim
rowGuides.sourceHint: Name one fact you can safely write and one claim you should not write yet.

need.label: Safe fact you can use
need.placeholder: State one fact the notes already support.

source.label: Source that supports it
source.placeholder: Example: Team PR status in Source Notes.

question.label: Unsafe claim to leave out
question.placeholder: Name one rumor, guess, or claim you should not write yet.

risk.label: Why to leave it out
risk.placeholder: Explain what proof is missing or why the claim is unsafe.

warmup_observation.title: Warmup Observation at Media Row
warmup_observation.content: You are at {{media row}} before the game starts. Open the observation popups in any order and keep one running note as you collect useful details about Reed. If something you see gives you a question for Reed or Coach Harris, write that question in the same note so you can ask it later.
warmup_observation.simulatedAction: Inspect in-arena information surfaces and build one running observation note before interviews.
warmup_observation.learningGoal: Keep four kinds of information separate: what the team officially says, what you see yourself, normal arena details, and rumors no one has confirmed.

physicalWorkDesign.realWorkflowScript: Arrive at media row, use the credential/deadline context, inspect the team PR status, Reed movement, closing-group drill, trainer contact, and rumor feed in any order, and keep one running observation note with clear attribution limits and later interview questions.

procedure: Add useful details to the same running note
Capture Reed or Coach Harris questions that the observation creates
Save and close popups in any order
Keep clear what each detail can and cannot support
stateChanges: Inspected surfaces are marked complete and the observation note is stored for grading.

actionClassification.mappingRationale: The student reads a visible source surface before using it.
The real job requires observation to become notes, not a hidden click result.

physicalWorkDesign.mainSceneRole: One 16:9 arena/media-row stage with court and work table hotspots.
popupSceneRoles: Readable closeups for PR email, Reed movement, closing-group drill, trainer contact, and rumor feed.
physicalWorkDesign.statePersistence: Inspected surfaces and the running reporter notes remain in the action log stored under warmup_observation.
physicalWorkDesign.antiButtonAudit: There is no generic complete-observation button; the player must inspect source surfaces and use the shared notes field in the popups.

background.prompt: Arena media-row view before an NBA game at Harbor City Arena. Foreground has a reporter work table with credential, notebook, phone, and a laptop open to a team PR injury-status email; midground shows Malik Reed warming up near the three-point line; a trainer stands near the baseline; empty lower-bowl seats and court lights fill the background. Leave clear clickable zones for the laptop PR email, phone feed, Reed movement, and trainer check-in.

coordinateMap.label: team PR injury email on laptop

backgrounds.prompt: Arena media-row view before an NBA game at Harbor City Arena. Foreground has a reporter work table with credential, notebook, phone, and a laptop open to a team PR injury-status email; midground shows Malik Reed warming up near the three-point line; a trainer stands near the baseline; empty lower-bowl seats and court lights fill the background. Leave clear clickable zones for the laptop PR email, phone feed, Reed movement, and trainer check-in.

readableSurfaces.prompt: Readable closeup of a laptop email from team PR. Text should include: "Malik Reed - Available. No minutes limit listed." Keep it realistic, like an email open on a reporter laptop at media row, not a printed note.

Closeup from media row of Malik Reed doing a hard lateral cut during warmups. Show athletic movement and a neutral facial expression, with no answer captions, checkmarks, conclusion labels, or documentation text.

Closeup from media row of Malik Reed taking late-clock defensive drill reps with four Cyclones teammates who look like the projected closing group. Coach Harris stands near the sideline with a clipboard, stopping one action and pointing Reed toward a weak-side switch. Show a realistic pregame drill, no answer captions, no arrows, no checkmarks, no conclusion labels, and no documentation text.

Closeup of Malik Reed briefly speaking with a trainer near the baseline during warmups. Show a routine check-in posture, phones and media nearby, with no answer captions, checkmarks, conclusion labels, or documentation text.

Readable phone closeup showing fan-account posts speculating about a Malik Reed trade. Make usernames look fictional and unofficial. Include visible uncertainty such as "hearing chatter" and "unconfirmed" but no verification badges.

readableSurfaces.label: Official team injury email
readableSurfaces.readableText: The email says Reed can play tonight and does not list a minutes limit. It does not say how his ankle feels, so do not write that he feels healthy unless Reed or the team says that.
readableSurfaces.playerInstruction: Read this as the team's official update. In your note, write that the source is {{Team PR}} and that Reed is listed as able to play.
readableSurfaces.observationPrompt: In your running note, write the official status and who it came from. Do not add any health claim the email does not say.
readableSurfaces.observationPlaceholder: Example: Team PR says Reed can play tonight. No minutes limit is listed. This does not prove how his ankle feels.

readableSurfaces.label: Reed movement in warmups
readableSurfaces.readableText: From {{media row}}, you see Reed move side to side, make one hard change of direction, and shoot from the corner. That is useful observation, but it is not proof that his ankle is fully healthy.
readableSurfaces.observationPrompt: In your running note, write the movement you saw and the question it gives you for Reed.
readableSurfaces.observationPlaceholder: Example: Reed moved side to side and made a hard change of direction. Ask him what that felt like.
readableSurfaces.expectedObservation: A strong note says Reed moved side to side and made a hard change of direction during warmups, then turns that into a later Reed question about what it felt like without treating the observation as proof that the ankle is fully healthy.
readableSurfaces.playerInstruction: Watch Reed's movement and write only what you can see. Add one fair question you could ask Reed later.

readableSurfaces.label: Final-minutes group drill
readableSurfaces.readableText: In this drill, Reed practices with four teammates who look like a possible {{closing group}}. Coach Harris stops one play, points Reed to a different defensive spot, and restarts the drill.
readableSurfaces.playerInstruction: Treat this as a clue, not a fact. In your note, write that Reed practiced with a group that might play near the end, then add a Coach Harris question about what it means.
readableSurfaces.observationPrompt: In your running note, write why this might matter and turn it into a Coach Harris question.
readableSurfaces.observationPlaceholder: Example: Reed practiced with a possible final-minutes group. Ask Coach Harris whether that means anything for tonight.

readableSurfaces.label: Trainer check-in
readableSurfaces.readableText: After the movement drill, Reed briefly talks with a team trainer, points at the outside of his right shoe, and goes back to warmups. No one announces any new status while you are watching.
readableSurfaces.playerInstruction: Write this only as something you saw. Do not call it an injury update unless the team or Reed says that.
readableSurfaces.observationPrompt: In your running note, write only what you saw. Mark it as a question, not a confirmed update.
readableSurfaces.observationPlaceholder: Example: Reed talked with a trainer and pointed at his shoe, then returned to warmups. Ask later if it was routine.

readableSurfaces.label: Fan rumor post
readableSurfaces.readableText: The phone post says it is "hearing chatter" about Reed's future and also says "unconfirmed." It is not from the team, the league, Reed, his agent, or someone willing to be named.
readableSurfaces.playerInstruction: Check who is saying it before you use it. In your note, write that this is a fan account and should stay out of the story unless a real source confirms it.
readableSurfaces.observationPrompt: In your running note, write where the claim came from and why it should stay out unless someone real confirms it.
readableSurfaces.observationPlaceholder: Example: Fan account says unconfirmed chatter about Reed. Leave it out unless a named source confirms it.

successConditions: Includes official team status in the running note set.
Turns Reed movement into a specific later Reed question.
Turns closing-group drill reps into a specific later Coach Harris question.
Recognizes trainer contact as observed context, not confirmed medical information.
failureModes: Treats warmup observation as official medical clearance.
Publishes fan rumor or hallway chatter as fact.

coach_availability.title: Coach Harris Availability
coach_availability.content:
Coach Len Harris is at the interview-room platform before tipoff. Before you press Start conversation, review the Support Notes panel, including your pregame reporting plan and warmup memo. Use those notes to prepare two short, focused questions. Do not turn what you hear into a lasting claim about Reed's health or the lineup yet.

Take notes below the conversation while you interview him. You will write the deadline article from your own interview notes, the conversation record, and the materials you collected in the workplace.
coach_availability.goalPrompt: You are Coach Len Harris of the fictional Harbor City Cyclones. Answer like a guarded but professional NBA coach in an in-person media availability. If the reporter asks about Reed status or closing lineup logic with concise, fair wording, say Reed is listed available and the staff will watch game flow, then give the usable quote: "Closing groups are situational. Tonight, if that group earns it, we will ride it." Do not say Reed is fully cleared, fully healed, back to normal, or medically cleared; Team PR owns official status language. If the reporter asks rumor, trade, full-health, or personality questions, do not confirm the premise and redirect to the game. After the reporter has asked two substantive questions or after four user turns, wrap up with a closing line and end the availability; do not ask follow-up questions.
coach_availability.meetingContext: The user is playing the reporter, an entry-level NBA beat reporter standing in the arena interview room before tipoff. They prepared a reporting plan and need a coach quote about Reed usage and the closing lineup.
coach_availability.playerGoal: In 2-4 turns, ask two short, focused pregame questions based on your support notes, without making a lasting claim about Reed's health or the lineup.
coach_availability.preStartPrompt: Before starting, read the Support Notes panel. Use your pregame reporting plan and warmup memo to prepare two short, focused questions.
coach_availability.notePrompt: Coach Harris notes for your article
coach_availability.noteHelper: Take notes during this availability. You will write the deadline article from your notes, so capture usable Coach Harris wording, accurate summaries, and anything that still needs Reed or postgame confirmation.
coach_availability.notePlaceholder: Example: Coach Harris said closing groups are situational... / Need Reed later on what he felt closing...
coach_availability.prepReferenceContent: Facts available: Team PR lists Reed as available with no minutes limit. Story question: whether Reed's return changes late-game usage. Risk: one pregame answer cannot prove a lasting lineup change. Avoid the unverified trade rumor.
coach_availability.imageBrief: In-person NBA pregame interview room. Coach Len Harris stands at a small riser with team backdrop while reporters hold recorders and phones. First-person reporter perspective, no laptop or video call grid.
coach_availability.endpoint: End once you have one usable, named coach answer and know what still needs Reed or postgame attribution.
coach_availability.successCriteria: You keep the question based on the available materials, avoid rumor language, and respect the limits of a pregame answer.

inputArtifacts.label: Pregame reporting plan
inputArtifacts.summary: Student reporting needs and questions from the prior plan.

inputArtifacts.label: Warmup observation memo
inputArtifacts.summary: Player-written running notes from the warmup observation scene.

inputArtifacts.label: Coach availability facts
inputArtifacts.summary: Reed official status, story question, and rumor/overclaiming risks.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Ask concise, attributable coach availability questions.
continuity: Coach answer informs possession watching, live note capture, and postgame questions.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Beat reporters routinely use short coach availability windows to clarify what can and cannot be reported before a game.

possession_timeline_watch.title: Possession Timeline Watch
possession_timeline_watch.content: You are back at media row for the final minutes. Watch every timeline moment. To finish this page, save completed notes for at least 3 different moments. A completed note means you pick one note label and write note text for that same moment. You can leave the other moments watched only. Use your notes to write better Reed questions than the box score alone would give you.
possession_timeline_watch.referenceTitle: What you know before the final stretch
possession_timeline_watch.referenceContent:
Pregame frame: Malik Reed is coming back from an ankle sprain, and Coach Harris said the closing group depends on the game situation.

Completion rule:
- Watch all timeline moments.
- Save at least 3 completed notes. Each one needs one label plus note text.

Reporting rules:
- Write down what you personally see.
- Mark what needs Reed or Coach Harris attribution before you publish it.
- Do not treat a rumor, body language, or one play as a confirmed medical or trade fact.
- Your notes will help you in the postgame scrum and deadline story.
possession_timeline_watch.summaryPrompt: After you have at least 3 completed notes, read the Reed question advice, then write two short Reed questions to ask after the game.

questionReference.rowTitle: Question for Reed after the game
questionReference.title: Pregame Reed question
questionReference.helper: You can reuse this question as one of your two questions, or update it now based on what you saw late in the game.

possession_timeline_watch.questionGuidance:
Good Reed questions should help you get a quote or clear source for tonight's story.

Before writing each question:
- Connect it to one specific late-game moment from your notes.
- Ask what Reed personally felt, noticed, or understood.
- Keep the wording fair. Do not sound like you already know the answer.
- If Coach Harris, Team PR, or another source should answer it, do not make Reed confirm it.
questionPlaceholders: Ask Reed about what he personally felt or noticed on a specific late possession.
Ask Reed for a usable quote that connects the timeline to tonight's story.

categories.label: Fact I can use
categories.description: A score, official stat, or play you saw clearly enough to use.

categories.label: Ask Reed
categories.description: Something Reed can explain after the game.

categories.label: Ask Coach
categories.description: A lineup, play plan, or playing-time question for Coach Harris.

categories.label: Scene detail
categories.description: A detail you saw or heard that makes the story feel real, but does not prove the main point by itself.

categories.label: Hold/check later
categories.description: Interesting, but not safe to publish until a stronger source confirms it.

events.headline: Reed checks back in with the closing group
events.description: Coach Harris sends Reed back into the game. Reed jogs in without an obvious limp, but he glances toward the trainer before the ball comes back into play.
events.notebookPrompt: Write one note about only what you saw. You may record Reed checking in, the trainer looking over, or both. Do not guess what the trainer glance means or say Reed is hurt or healthy unless a named source says it.

events.headline: Denver makes Reed fight through a screen
events.description: Denver uses a teammate's screen to slow Reed down. Reed slides sideways, gets back in front, and challenges the shot without grabbing at his ankle.
events.notebookPrompt: Write the note: this is something you saw yourself. Record what happened, then decide who needs to confirm how it felt.

events.headline: Denver's bench group finishes a 13-2 run
events.description: The Cyclones bench looks quiet during the timeout. Coach Harris keeps Reed with the closing group and sends the same five players back out.
events.notebookPrompt: Write the note: is this part of a bench-problem story, closing-group context, or just background?

events.headline: Reed draws help and finds the open corner
events.description: Reed drives, pulls a second defender toward him, and passes to the open corner for a three. The arena shifts from nervous to loud.
events.notebookPrompt: Write the note: this is a useful basketball detail. Decide whether it is a fact you can use, a scene detail, or something to ask after the game.

events.headline: A fan message pushes a trade rumor
events.description: While play is stopped, your phone buzzes with an unverified message from a fan account. It claims another team asked the Cyclones whether Reed could be traded. No team, agent, league, or named source is included.
events.notebookPrompt: Write the note: this may be noise, not news. Decide whether it belongs anywhere in your working notes before the final buzzer.

events.headline: Reed guards Denver's final screen
events.description: Denver tries to make Reed defend the last action. Reed stays in front long enough to force a late pass, and the Cyclones grab the rebound that seals the 112-107 win.
events.notebookPrompt: Write the note: this is likely part of the article's main angle. Record the play clearly and think about one specific Reed question.

events.headline: Final score board confirms the game facts
events.description: The arena result board shows Harbor City Cyclones 112, Denver Altitude 107. The official box score gives Malik Reed 24 points, 6 assists, and 2 steals.
events.notebookPrompt: Write the note: this is the confirmed final score and Reed's official stat line. Record the result and box score before walking into the scrum.

deskWorkDesign.workSurface: Interactive possession timeline from a media-row view

inputArtifacts.label: Pregame coach availability
inputArtifacts.summary: Coach Harris framed closing groups as situational

inputArtifacts.label: Final-stretch possessions
inputArtifacts.summary: Six late possessions plus the in-arena final result board involving Reed, lineup usage, rumor noise, and official postgame facts

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Watch late possessions, write useful notes, and draft two Reed scrum questions.
continuity: Timeline notes feed the Malik Reed postgame scrum and deadline story.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: The scene makes the player watch and filter live game events rather than receiving a finished postgame packet.

media_scrum.title: Malik Reed Postgame Scrum
media_scrum.content: After the game, Malik Reed is standing near the {{baseline}} while reporters gather around him for a short in-person {{scrum}}. You already wrote two Reed questions. Use this moment to ask them clearly and take notes while he answers.
media_scrum.goalPrompt: You are Malik Reed, veteran guard for the fictional Harbor City Cyclones. You are standing in a quick postgame media scrum after a 112-107 win. Sound like a real tired but professional athlete: brief, human, guarded with medical details, and more open when the reporter asks fair, specific basketball questions. If the reporter greets you, acknowledge it and invite the question without adding facts. If the question is vague, answer with a short general line about feeling good, competing, helping the group, or taking it play by play. If the reporter asks a precise, fair question, reveal one useful detail at a time across separate answers: the ankle felt stable on hard cuts, the training staff removed the minutes limit this morning, halftime trainer check-ins were routine, you expected to close if the defense held, and you do not want the night framed only as a personal comeback. If the reporter asks multiple questions at once, answer the most relevant part first and say you can take the second part quickly if there is time. If they repeat a question, briefly restate without adding a new fact unless the wording is more precise. If the question is confusing or speech-to-text seems garbled, ask for a quick repeat or clarify the part you heard. If the question is off topic, pull it back to the game, your role, or the closing stretch. If they ask about rumors, trades, full health, or any claim that overstates what is known, do not confirm it; say you are focused on tonight and that official or roster matters belong to the team. If the question asks for Coach Harris's tactics or Team PR's official status, answer only your personal view and say Coach Harris or Team PR owns the final confirmation. If the reporter is rude or inappropriate, stay calm, set a boundary, and return to basketball. Do not mention being an AI, model, or simulator. Do not volunteer everything at once. After the reporter has asked two substantive questions or after four user turns, wrap up with a natural closing line and end the scrum; do not continue opening new topics.
media_scrum.meetingContext: The user is playing the reporter, an entry-level NBA beat reporter in an in-person postgame arena scrum. They prepared two Reed questions from the possession timeline. Their job is to ask those questions one at a time, listen for one useful answer, and take notes for the deadline article.
media_scrum.playerGoal: Ask Reed the two questions you already wrote from your possession notes.
media_scrum.preStartPrompt: Before you start: read Question 1 and Question 2 in your prepared notes. Ask one question at a time. Listen for one useful quote or detail, write it in your notes, then end the conversation.
media_scrum.notePrompt: Malik Reed notes for your article
media_scrum.noteHelper: Use these notes with the {{conversation record}} when you write the {{deadline article}}. Capture Reed's exact words when you can, what he can personally confirm, and anything that still needs Coach Harris, Team PR, or another source.
media_scrum.notePlaceholder: Example: Reed said... / Quote or summary I can use... / Hold: needs Coach Harris or PR...
media_scrum.imageBrief: In-person NBA postgame media scrum near the baseline. Reporters hold phones and recorders toward veteran guard Malik Reed in team gear, with court lights, camera operators, and the tunnel behind him. No video call grid or laptop screen.
media_scrum.endpoint: Finish after you ask both prepared questions and save at least one useful Reed answer in your notes.
media_scrum.successCriteria: A good interview has two clear Reed questions, one useful answer, and notes you can use in the article.

inputArtifacts.label: Prepared Reed questions
inputArtifacts.summary: Student's two Reed questions from the possession timeline.

inputArtifacts.label: Possession timeline notes
inputArtifacts.summary: Student's observed late-game notes, including the official result board from the final timeline event.

deskWorkDesign.studentAction: Ask the two prepared Reed postgame scrum questions and take notes on Reed's answers.
continuity: Scrum transcript feeds the deadline story.
deskWorkDesign.authenticityCheck: Short postgame scrums are a central beat-reporting moment under deadline pressure.

fast_gamer.title: Write the Game Article
fast_gamer.content: It is 10:34 PM. Tessa needs the top of the article now. Use Player Notes and Results in the CMS source panel. Do not use anything that is not shown there.
fast_gamer.prompt: Build the game article in four parts. For each part, compare the two paragraph choices, use the source panel, and drag or choose the paragraph that is safer and stronger for readers.
fast_gamer.headlinePrompt: Write one clear headline for the article.

articleSections.label: News Lead
articleSections.instruction: Start with the result and the main Reed angle.

choices.label: Choice A
choices.sourceHint: Supported by Results and final-minutes notes.
choices.text: HARBOR CITY - Malik Reed's return gave Harbor City the late-game steadiness it needed Wednesday, as the Cyclones closed out a 112-107 win over the Denver Altitude with Reed on the floor for the decisive stretch.

choices.label: Choice B
choices.sourceHint: Vague and not focused on the news.
choices.text: It was an exciting basketball game with many important moments, and fans had plenty to cheer about before the Cyclones finished with a victory.

articleSections.label: Proof
articleSections.instruction: Give the reader the confirmed score and Reed facts.

choices.label: Choice A
choices.sourceHint: Use Results for the score and stat line.
choices.text: Reed finished with 24 points, 6 assists, and 2 steals, according to the official box score. The team listed him available, and the official result confirmed his production in the win.

choices.label: Choice B
choices.sourceHint: Repeats the same numbers without adding useful proof.
choices.text: Reed had 24 points, 6 assists, and 2 steals. Those numbers were important because they showed he had 24 points, 6 assists, and 2 steals in the game.

articleSections.label: Angle
articleSections.instruction: Explain why the Reed minutes matter beyond the box score.

choices.label: Choice A
choices.text: The bigger story was not only that Reed played. It was that Harbor City trusted him in the final minutes, including late possessions that protected a tight lead after Denver cut into the margin.

choices.label: Choice B
choices.sourceHint: One game does not prove a permanent rotation.
choices.text: Harris has clearly found his permanent closing lineup, and Reed's return locks the Cyclones into the same five-man group for future close games.

articleSections.label: Close
articleSections.instruction: End with a careful attribution or limitation, not a guess.

choices.label: Choice A
choices.sourceHint: Use only if Coach Harris notes are saved.
choices.text: Coach Len Harris did not turn one night into a permanent lineup promise. He called closing groups situational, which supports a cautious frame: Reed gave the Cyclones a late option, but the rotation question remains open.

choices.label: Choice B
choices.sourceHint: No named source confirms trade talk.
choices.text: The win will only increase trade speculation around Reed, whose return came while scouts and fan accounts were already buzzing about possible deadline movement.

cmsRequirements.label: Reed return
cmsRequirements.sourceHint: Use Results and saved Player Notes

cmsRequirements.label: Final-minutes lineup angle
cmsRequirements.sourceHint: Use Coach Harris or Possession Timeline

cmsRequirements.label: One sourced quote or summary
cmsRequirements.sourceHint: Use Coach Harris or Malik Reed if saved

cmsRequirements.label: Trade rumors
cmsRequirements.sourceHint: Leave out unless a named source confirms

responseSources.title: Warmup Observation Notes
responseSources.safeUse: What you personally saw before the game: official status, movement, drill reps, trainer contact, and rumor context.
responseSources.caution: Observation can create questions, but it does not prove Reed is fully healthy or confirm a permanent lineup decision.
responseSources.emptyText: No warmup observation note is saved.

responseSources.title: Coach Harris Interview Notes
responseSources.safeUse: Coach Harris can support late-game lineup logic, what the staff was watching, and cautious role language.
responseSources.caution: One pregame answer should not become a permanent rotation claim or a full medical conclusion.
responseSources.emptyText: No Coach Harris interview note is saved.

responseSources.title: Final-Minutes Notes for Article
responseSources.safeUse: Use only confirmed final-stretch observations that help explain Reed's late role. Use Results for the final score and stat line.
responseSources.caution: Leave out rumors and any medical claim stronger than your named sources support.
responseSources.emptyText: No possession timeline note is saved.

responseSources.title: Malik Reed Scrum Notes
responseSources.safeUse: Reed can support his own experience, what he felt on specific plays, and usable quote or summary material.
responseSources.caution: Reed should not be used to confirm team PR, Coach Harris tactics, or trade rumors unless he directly says it.
responseSources.emptyText: No Malik Reed scrum note is saved.

cmsGuidance.title: Need a structure?
cmsGuidance.summary: Use this only if you are stuck. It shows what each article part should do.

items.label: News Lead
items.detail: Start with the win and Reed's late role, not the rumor.

items.label: Proof
items.detail: Use Results for the final score and stat line.

items.label: Angle
items.detail: Explain why Reed's final minutes matter.

items.label: Close
items.detail: End with careful attribution, not a rumor or guess.

appTabs.label: Results
appTabs.content:
Official result

Final: Harbor City Cyclones 112, Denver Altitude 107.

Official box score

Malik Reed finished with 24 points, 6 assists, and 2 steals.

grading.title: End of Game Night

grading.imageBrief: A calm end-of-day workplace wrap-up moment with the submitted story complete, relevant notes settled, and no assessment results or score cards visible.

final_report.title: Final Report

assessment_gate.title: The Day Is Complete

assessment_gate.imageBrief: A calm end-of-day workplace wrap-up moment with the student stepping back from the finished work, relevant tools or documents settled, and warm late-day light. No assessment results or score cards are visible.

malik.name: Malik Reed
malik.persona: Malik is a respected veteran guard returning from an ankle sprain. He is media-savvy, careful with medical details, and more open when a reporter asks precise, fair questions about the game instead of pushing him toward a dramatic comeback narrative.

coach.name: Len Harris
coach.persona: Len Harris is a veteran NBA head coach who protects competitive details but respects reporters who ask concise, fair, basketball-specific questions. He avoids rumor, dislikes personality framing, and gives usable quotes when the question is tied to lineup logic or game context.

tessa.name: Tessa Ortiz
tessa.role: Senior NBA Editor, Full Court Wire
tessa.persona: Tessa is a sharp national NBA editor who cares about speed, attribution, and clean story framing. She likes young reporters who are direct about what they know, honest about what they do not know, and disciplined enough to leave unverified material out of copy.

intro.welcomeBody: Step into an entry-level NBA beat reporter's game night: choose the story angle, observe from the arena, ask questions in person, sort messy notes, write fast, and protect accuracy under deadline pressure.

steps.label: Frame
steps.content: Choose a story angle that fits the game, the audience, and what you can prove.

steps.label: Observe
steps.content: Record what you can see in the arena, and keep it separate from official status.

steps.label: Ask
steps.content: Use limited coach and player access to ask focused questions that give you a clear source.

steps.label: File
steps.content: Write and communicate clearly enough for an editor to publish before the deadline.

glossary.beat: A regular reporting assignment focused on one team, league, institution, or topic. An NBA beat reporter follows the same team often and learns its patterns over time.
glossary.gamer: A same-day game story or recap. It is usually written quickly after the final buzzer and updated with postgame quotes or analysis.
glossary.nut graf: The paragraph near the top of a story that explains the main point and why it matters.
glossary.scrum: A group interview where several reporters ask questions around a player or coach, often with very little time.
glossary.baseline: The court line behind each basket. In this scene, Reed is standing near that area while reporters gather around him.
glossary.possession timeline: The late-game play-by-play notes you just made. Use them to ask Reed about specific moments instead of asking a vague question.
glossary.deadline article: The game story you must write quickly after the final buzzer so an editor can publish it on time.
glossary.source tray: The CMS panel that holds your saved notes, official result, and source checks while you write.
glossary.final-minutes lineup angle: The story point about whether Reed's return changed who played at the end of the close game.
glossary.medical claim: A statement about Reed's health or recovery. Only write it if Team PR, Reed, or another named source supports it.
glossary.conversation record: The transcript of what you and Reed said during the interview. It helps you check quotes and remember what he confirmed.
glossary.availability: A scheduled time when players or coaches are available to reporters before or after practice, shootaround, or games.
glossary.on the record: Information you can quote or describe publicly with the source named or clearly identified.
glossary.postgame: After the game ends. A postgame question is asked once the game is over and players or coaches talk to reporters.
glossary.named source: A person or official document you can clearly identify, such as Team PR, Coach Harris, Reed, or the arena media schedule.
glossary.claim: Something someone says is true. In this simulation, do not write a claim as fact unless a named source or your own clear observation supports it.
glossary.attribution: Words that tell readers where information came from, such as a player quote, team announcement, public record, or your own observation.
glossary.Team PR: The team's public relations staff. They send official team updates to reporters, such as injury status or interview times.
glossary.color: Observed scene detail that makes a story feel alive, such as body language, crowd noise, or arena atmosphere. It supports verified reporting but does not replace it.
glossary.copy desk: Editors who check stories for accuracy, grammar, style, clarity, headlines, and readiness to publish.
glossary.closing group: The players a coach trusts to use near the end of a close game. A warmup drill can be a clue, but it does not prove who will finish the game.
glossary.minutes limit: A limit on how much a player is expected or allowed to play, often after an injury.
glossary.CMS: Content Management System. The website tool reporters and editors use to draft, edit, and publish articles.
glossary.media row: The work area at a game where reporters sit, watch, take notes, and file stories.
glossary.box score: The official game summary with the final score and player stats, such as points, assists, and steals.

sample_answers.pregame_plan: [{"rowTitle":"Question for Coach Harris before the game","need":"Whether Reed can play near the end of a close game.","question":"With Malik listed available and no minutes limit listed, what will decide whether he can play near the end of a close game?","risk":"This answer explains the basketball decision without guessing about Reed's health or a permanent rotation change."},{"rowTitle":"Question for Reed after the game","need":"How Reed felt when the ankle had to respond at game speed.","question":"What was the first play tonight when the ankle had to respond to a hard cut or defensive slide?","risk":"This gives Reed's own account instead of turning observation into a health claim."},{"rowTitle":"Safe fact and unsafe claim","need":"Reed is listed available after a right ankle sprain, with no minutes limit listed.","source":"Team PR official injury report","question":"Social media trade-rumor chatter about Reed.","risk":"Leave it out because there is no team, agent, league, or named source confirming it."}]
sample_answers.warmup_observation: Team PR lists Reed available with no minutes limit. From media row, Reed went through lateral slides, a hard right-to-left cut, and corner threes; ask Reed later what he felt on that first hard cut rather than turning the observation into a health claim. Reed also took late-clock defensive drill reps with the projected closing group; ask Coach Harris whether that reflects a possible closing group or just warmup structure. Trainer check-in after the lateral drill is observed context only, not a status update. Fan-account rumor is unconfirmed and should stay out unless verified.
sample_answers.possession_timeline_summary: 1) Ask Reed how the ankle felt when Denver forced him through the high screen and on the final switch. 2) Ask how he read his role in the closing group after the bench run and corner assist.
sample_answers.live_game_note_rationale: The final-stretch note creates new postgame scrum questions because Reed playing the final 6:12 makes the closing lineup the game angle. The fan-account DM stays out because it has no named or on-record source.
sample_answers.fast_gamer:
Headline: Reed return steadies Cyclones late

HARBOR CITY - Malik Reed's return gave Harbor City the late-game steadiness it needed Wednesday, as the Cyclones closed out a 112-107 win over the Denver Altitude with Reed on the floor for the decisive stretch.

Reed finished with 24 points, 6 assists, and 2 steals, according to the official box score. The team listed him available, and the official result confirmed his production in the win.

The bigger story was not only that Reed played. It was that Harbor City trusted him in the final minutes, including late possessions that protected a tight lead after Denver cut into the margin.

Coach Len Harris did not turn one night into a permanent lineup promise. He called closing groups situational, which supports a cautious frame: Reed gave the Cyclones a late option, but the rotation question remains open.
sample_answers.coach_availability: Ask: Coach, with Malik available and no minutes limit listed, what will determine whether he can close tonight? Ask: What do you need from that closing group defensively against Denver? Follow-up: Is this a matchup decision or something you are evaluating longer term?
sample_answers.media_scrum: Ask: Malik, what was the first play tonight when you felt the ankle had to respond at game speed? Ask: Did the staff give you any cap or check-in point before you could close? Ask: When Coach went back to you late, what did that group give you defensively? Ask: Is there anything about the ankle you still need to manage tomorrow?

freeTextResponses.possession_timeline_summary: 1) Ask Reed how the ankle felt when Denver forced him through the high screen and on the final switch. 2) Ask how he read his role in the closing group after the bench run and corner assist.
freeTextResponses.possession_timeline_notes: {"viewedEventIds":["denver_high_screen","bench_run_context","reed_corner_three_assist","final_high_screen","final_score_board"],"notes":{"denver_high_screen":{"categoryId":"ask_reed","note":"Reed recovered over a high screen without obvious hesitation; ask him what the first hard cut felt like."},"bench_run_context":{"categoryId":"ask_coach","note":"Coach Harris kept the same five after Denver run; ask whether the closing group was matchup-specific."},"reed_corner_three_assist":{"categoryId":"confirmed_fact","note":"Reed drew help and assisted the corner three during a tied late possession."},"final_high_screen":{"categoryId":"confirmed_fact","note":"Reed stayed in front on the final high-screen action before the rebound sealed the win."},"final_score_board":{"categoryId":"confirmed_fact","note":"Final board shows Cyclones 112, Denver 107; official box score credits Reed with 24 points, 6 assists, and 2 steals."}},"reedQuestions":["How did the ankle feel when Denver forced you through the high screen and on the final switch?","How did you read your role in the closing group after the bench run and corner assist?"],"summary":"1) Ask Reed how the ankle felt when Denver forced him through the high screen and on the final switch.\n2) Ask how he read his role in the closing group after the bench run and corner assist."}

criteria.name: Story Angle Selection
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: Chooses a reportable angle based on news value, verification, deadline fit, and reader meaning instead of rumor. Score only this task; do not use later notes, prep work, or downstream artifacts as evidence. 7-8: The main elements are present, but one small gap in evidence, specificity, priority, notes, or response quality remains. 4-6: Some work is useful, but multiple important decisions, checks, explanations, notes, or next actions are missing. 0-3: The response misses the task goal, ignores evidence, creates risky claims or confusion, or is not usable.

criteria.name: Reporting Plan
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: The plan gives specific Coach Harris and Reed questions, identifies a confirmed fact and source, and names one unconfirmed claim to leave out with a clear reason. Score only this planning task; do not use later notes, interviews, or article work as evidence. 7-8: The main elements are present, but one small gap in evidence, specificity, priority, notes, or response quality remains. 4-6: Some work is useful, but multiple important decisions, checks, explanations, notes, or next actions are missing. 0-3: The response misses the task goal, ignores evidence, creates risky claims or confusion, or is not usable.

criteria.name: Warmup Observation
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: Separates official information, observed movement, rumor, and medical claims, and clearly distinguishes publishable facts from material to hold or verify. Score only this observation task; do not use later prep notes or artifacts as evidence. If a safety-critical source distinction is skipped, do not award full credit even if other elements are strong. 7-8: The main elements are present, but one small gap in evidence, specificity, priority, notes, or response quality remains. 4-6: Some work is useful, but multiple important decisions, checks, explanations, notes, or next actions are missing. 0-3: The response misses the task goal, ignores evidence, creates risky claims or confusion, or is not usable.

criteria.name: Coach Harris Questioning
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: In the coach availability transcript, asks short, specific coach-appropriate questions and avoids asking Coach Harris to answer for Reed personally or confirm unsupported claims. Score only this interview task; do not use later notes or article work as evidence. 7-8: The main elements are present, but one small gap in evidence, specificity, priority, notes, or response quality remains. 4-6: Some work is useful, but multiple important decisions, checks, explanations, notes, or next actions are missing. 0-3: The response misses the task goal, ignores evidence, creates risky claims or confusion, or is not usable.

criteria.name: Player Scrum Questioning
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: In the player scrum transcript, asks player-friendly, answerable questions that can produce attributable story material without pressure, leading wording, or unsupported health claims. Score only this interview task; do not use later article work as evidence. 7-8: The main elements are present, but one small gap in evidence, specificity, priority, notes, or response quality remains. 4-6: Some work is useful, but multiple important decisions, checks, explanations, notes, or next actions are missing. 0-3: The response misses the task goal, ignores evidence, creates risky claims or confusion, or is not usable.

criteria.name: Game Observation Notes
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: Organizes late-game events, observations, stats, usable scenes, and hold-back items in a form that supports a deadline game story. Score only this possession timeline task; do not use later interview or article work as evidence. 7-8: The main elements are present, but one small gap in evidence, specificity, priority, notes, or response quality remains. 4-6: Some work is useful, but multiple important decisions, checks, explanations, notes, or next actions are missing. 0-3: The response misses the task goal, ignores evidence, creates risky claims or confusion, or is not usable.

criteria.name: Deadline Game Article
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: Uses only confirmed facts and interview material, selects safe and strong paragraphs for News Lead, Proof, Angle, and Close, and writes a clear deadline headline. Score only this article assembly task; do not use unrelated notes as evidence. 7-8: The main elements are present, but one small gap in evidence, specificity, priority, notes, or response quality remains. 4-6: Some work is useful, but multiple important decisions, checks, explanations, notes, or next actions are missing. 0-3: The response misses the task goal, ignores evidence, creates risky claims or confusion, or is not usable.
